---
title: "Publishing data to EDI"
subtitle: "Using the HRL publication workflow with `{hrlpub}` and EMLassemblyline"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: false
    theme: flatly
---

## Overview

This guide walks through the HRL workflow for publishing a dataset to the [Environmental Data Initiative (EDI)](https://edirepository.org/) data repository. EDI hosts ecological and environmental datasets and makes them publicly findable and reusable through standardized metadata. This guide is for standard flat files where variable names are columns and observations are rows. You may be publishing multiple data tables within one EDI package.

The workflow follows [FAIR principles](https://www.go-fair.org/fair-principles/) — data should be **F**indable, **A**ccessible, **I**nteroperable, and **R**eusable. EDI achieves this through a metadata standard called **EML (Ecological Metadata Language)**, an XML format that documents who collected the data, where, when, how, and under what rights.

The [example](https://github.com/lucy-dwr/edi-hrl-pub-example) used throughout this guide is a dataset of fish microhabitat observations from the Feather River, collected by the California Department of Water Resources (DWR) and published as part of the Healthy Rivers and Landscapes Science Program. 

> **Note:** HRL Data Producers are expected to publish most datasets to EDI. However, very large or complex datasets—such as high-resolution imagery collections, climate model outputs, or large geospatial files—may exceed EDI’s size and storage constraints. These datasets will be hosted and published by the Central Data Team using HRL program infrastructure, with EDI metadata records and landing pages cross-linking to the hosted assets when appropriate. Additionally, there are some cases where data are not for public released and therefore would not be published.

---

### Pipeline at a glance

The publication workflow has four stages that run in order:

```
data/raw/  →  data/clean/  →  QC review  →  EDI
  ingest        clean            qc         publish
```

| Stage | Folder | What it does |
|-------|--------|--------------|
| **Ingest** | `ingest/` | Pulls raw data from APIs or file sources into `data/raw/` |
| **Clean** | `clean/` | Standardizes and fixes raw data; writes to `data/clean/` |
| **QC** | `qc/` | Validates cleaned data and flags suspect records |
| **Publish** | `publish/` | Generates EML metadata and submits to EDI |

---

## Prerequisites

### Install packages

This repository uses [`{renv}`](https://rstudio.github.io/renv/) to lock all package versions, so anyone who clones it gets an identical environment. Run this once from the project root in your R console:

```r
source("scripts/setup.R")
```

This calls `renv::restore()`, which installs all required packages into a project-local library without touching your system-wide R packages. Key packages used are:

- **`{hrlpub}`** — HRL's internal package wrapping EMLassemblyline for metadata generation and EDI submission
- **`{EMLassemblyline}`** — assembles EML XML from template files
- **`{readr}`, `{dplyr}`, `{stringr}`, `{lubridate}`** — tidyverse packages for data cleaning

### EDI account

To publish to EDI you need a free account at <https://portal.edirepository.org/>. You will also need to reserve a package ID before generating metadata (see [Step 4: Publish](#publish)).

---

## Repository structure

```
project/
├── data/
│   ├── raw/          # Raw, untouched data — never edited by hand
│   └── clean/        # Cleaned data and diagnostic outputs
├── ingest/           # Scripts to pull data from APIs or downloads
├── clean/            # Cleaning scripts
├── qc/               # Quality-control scripts and reports
├── publish/
│   ├── metadata/     # Metadata template files (you fill these in)
│   │   └── attributes/  # Column-level attribute definitions (one CSV per table)
│   ├── eml/          # Generated EML XML output
│   ├── make-eml.R    # Generates the EML file
│   └── publish.R     # Submits to EDI (disabled by default)
└── scripts/
    ├── setup.R       # Restores the renv environment
    └── run_pipeline.R  # Runs all four stages in sequence
```

Each stage folder contains two scripts:

- A **tutorial script** (e.g., `clean/clean-microhabitat-observations.R`) — step-by-step, with detailed comments; read and run this interactively when learning.
- A **runner function** (e.g., `clean/clean.R`) — exposes `run_clean()` for use by the pipeline runner or `{targets}`.

---

## Step 1: Ingest {#ingest}

**Script:** `ingest/read-data-cdec.R`

The ingest step pulls raw data from an external source and writes it to `data/raw/`. The example fetches 2024 precipitation data from the [CDEC API](https://cdec.water.ca.gov) using the `{cder}` package.

Two important design patterns are demonstrated here:

**Retry logic.** Public APIs can fail transiently. Wrapping the call in a retry loop avoids requiring manual re-runs:

```r
fetch_cdec_group_with_retry <- function(groups, start.date, end.date,
                                         max_attempts = 5, wait_seconds = 5) {
  for (attempt in seq_len(max_attempts)) {
    result <- tryCatch(
      cder::cdec_query_group(groups = groups,
                              start.date = start.date,
                              end.date = end.date),
      error = identity
    )
    if (!inherits(result, "error")) return(result)
    Sys.sleep(wait_seconds)
  }
  stop("Unable to fetch CDEC data after retries.", call. = FALSE)
}
```

**Idempotent downloads.** Re-running the script skips the download when output already exists, saving time and avoiding unnecessary API calls:

```r
if (file.exists(output_file) && !isTRUE(overwrite)) {
  message("Skipping download; output already exists: ", output_file)
  return(output_file)
}
```

**Output:** `data/raw/oroville_precip_2024_raw.rds`

> **Note:** Raw data for the microhabitat dataset (`data/raw/microhabitat_observations_raw.csv`) is included directly in the repository because it was provided as a static file rather than an API. For most datasets you'll either include a static file or write an ingest script like this one.

---

## Step 2: Clean {#clean}

**Script:** `clean/clean-microhabitat-observations.R`

The cleaning step reads raw data, applies a series of standardization checks, and writes the result to `data/clean/`. Every change is logged in an issue summary file, which documents data quality and provenance for reviewers.

### Date parsing

Raw date fields often contain multiple formats across rows. The script profiles all format variants before parsing:

```r
raw <- raw |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    date_format_raw = dplyr::case_when(
      stringr::str_detect(date, "^\\d{4}-\\d{2}-\\d{2}$") ~ "yyyy-mm-dd",
      stringr::str_detect(date, "^\\d{2}/\\d{2}/\\d{4}$") ~ "mm/dd/yyyy",
      stringr::str_detect(date, "^\\d{2}-\\d{2}-\\d{4}$") ~ "dd-mm-yyyy",
      is.na(date)                                          ~ "missing",
      TRUE                                                 ~ "other"
    )
  )
```

Dates are then parsed with multiple format fallbacks using `lubridate::parse_date_time()`. Rows that fail to parse are left as `NA` and logged.

### Negative value and percent range checks

`hrlpub::run_clean_checks()` scans declared numeric columns for values that should never be negative (e.g., fish count, depth) and flags percent columns outside the 0–100 range. Negative values are set to `NA`:

```r
negative_cols <- c("count", "fl_mm", "dist_to_bottom", "depth",
                    "focal_velocity", "velocity")

clean_checks <- hrlpub::run_clean_checks(
  data = raw,
  dataset_name = "microhabitat",
  negative_cols = negative_cols,
  percent_cols = percent_cols
)

# Set negatives to NA
raw <- raw |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    dplyr::across(dplyr::all_of(negative_cols),
                  ~ replace(.x, !is.na(.x) & .x < 0, NA))
  )
```

### Controlled vocabulary standardization

Species names and geomorphic unit labels are standardized to a controlled vocabulary. A lookup table maps known misspellings and variants to their canonical forms:

```r
species_map <- tibble::tribble(
  ~raw_value,                    ~clean_value,
  "chinok salmon",              "chinook salmon",
  "steelhead trout (wlid)",     "steelhead trout (wild)",
  "steelhead trout, (clipped)", "steelhead trout (clipped)",
  "steelhed trout (wild)",      "steelhead trout (wild)",
  # ... etc.
)

raw <- raw |>
  dplyr::mutate(species_norm = species |>
    stringr::str_replace_all(",", "") |>
    stringr::str_squish() |>
    stringr::str_to_lower()) |>
  dplyr::left_join(species_map, by = c("species_norm" = "raw_value")) |>
  dplyr::mutate(species = dplyr::coalesce(clean_value, species_norm))
```

Values that don't match the controlled vocabulary after normalization are set to `NA` and logged as unrecognized.

### Outputs

```
data/clean/
├── microhabitat_observations_clean.csv       # The cleaned dataset
├── microhabitat_observations_issue_summary.csv  # Summary of all issues found
└── diagnostics/
    ├── microhabitat_date_format_counts.csv
    ├── microhabitat_date_parse_failures.csv
    ├── microhabitat_species_standardized_details.csv
    ├── microhabitat_species_missing_or_unrecognized.csv
    ├── microhabitat_channel_standardized_details.csv
    └── microhabitat_channel_missing_or_unrecognized.csv
```

---

## Step 3: Quality Control {#qc}

**Script:** `qc/qc-microhabitat-observations.R`

Quality control refers to data cleaning and checks that are performed after data has been collected and stored. The are multiple sources of data issues anywhere from equipment failures to data entry typos. QC is performed to remove known data issues to the extent possible.

The QC step validates the cleaned data using `hrlpub::run_qc_checks()`, which applies standardized checks for fish observation records. This function is designed to run basic QC checks and it is expected that additional checks will be run and documented.

Each row is assigned a flag:

| Flag | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `PASS` | Record meets all QC criteria |
| `SUSPECT` | Record is unusual but may be valid |
| `REJECT` | Record likely contains an error |

```r
qc_results <- hrlpub::run_qc_checks(
  data = qc_data,
  data_type = "fish_observation",
  max_count = 1000          # flag counts above this threshold
)

qc_report <- hrlpub::generate_qc_report(
  qc_results = qc_results,
  output_file = "data/clean/diagnostics/microhabitat_qc_report.rds"
)
```

### Outputs

```
data/clean/
├── microhabitat_observations_qc_issue_summary.csv
└── diagnostics/
    ├── microhabitat_qc_flags.csv           # Per-row flag details
    ├── microhabitat_qc_flag_reason_counts.csv
    └── microhabitat_qc_report.rds
```

Review the flag details before proceeding to publication. SUSPECT and REJECT records should be investigated and either corrected or documented.

---

## Step 4: Publish {#publish}

The publication step has two parts: generating the EML metadata file, and submitting to EDI.

### Fill in metadata templates

Before generating the EML file, fill in the metadata files in `publish/metadata/`. These are the inputs that describe your dataset to EDI and to anyone who later discovers or uses it.

| File | What to provide |
|------|-----------------|
| `abstract.txt` | A plain-text paragraph describing the study and dataset |
| `keywords.txt` | Tab-separated keywords for discovery (one per line). It is required to include `Healthy Rivers and Landscapes` as a keyword to ensure all datasets in this program are linked. |
| `personnel/personnel.csv` | Dataset creators and contacts; one row per person-role combination |
| `geographic_coverage.txt` | Bounding box coordinates (N/S/E/W) and a place name |
| `intellectual_rights.txt` | License text; CC BY applies to most HRL datasets |
| `methods.docx` | Free-text description of field and lab methods |
| `taxonomic_coverage.txt` | Scientific names, authority system (e.g., ITIS), and authority IDs — only needed if the dataset includes species observations. You can use the `taxonomyCleanr` package to help find the `authority_id` for species in your dataset. |
| `custom_units.txt` | Definitions for any units not in the standard EML unit dictionary |
| `attributes/attributes_<table>.csv` | Column-by-column definitions for each data table: name, description, class, units, missing value codes |

#### Abstract

Abstracts should be clearly written and include specific information about the data. Some tips to writing a good abstract: begin with the what/where/when, be specific about the data contents (e.g. number of sites, types of measurements, key variables), describe the spatial and temporal extent clearly, briefly mention the methods, state the purpose or application for the data.

#### Attribute definitions

Each data table needs a data dictionary (corresponding attributes file in `publish/metadata/attributes/`). These will ultimately be saved as .txt files though this will be handled in the EML creation. Complete a template for each data table using the csv template. This is one of the most important pieces of metadata because it ensures data are being used appropriately. Include as much detail as possible to describe a variable in the data table. 

Valid `class` values are `numeric`, `character`, `Date`, `categorical`. The `unit` column uses the [EML unit dictionary](https://knb.ecoinformatics.org/emlparser/docs/eml-2.2.0/eml-unitTypeDefinitions.html); any units not listed there must be defined in `custom_units.txt`.

#### Methods

This document should contain information about your data collection that is important to how the data are used. You may have thorough Standard Operating Protocol (SOP) documents. These should be stored in the GitHub repository but not published. The methods document is clear and concise, and ideally highlights the most important components of your methodology while linking to more detailed documentation.

#### Personnel

The `personnel/personnel.csv` file lists dataset creators and contacts. A person can appear more than once with different roles:

```
givenName, surName, organizationName, electronicMailAddress, role
Jane, Smith, My Organization, jane@example.org, creator
Jane, Smith, My Organization, jane@example.org, contact
```

See [EMLassemblyline docs](https://ediorg.github.io/EMLassemblyline/articles/edit_tmplts.html) for more details on roles. "creator" and "contact" roles are required. Similar to attributes, these will ultimately be saved as .txt files. 

> **Tip:** Edit the CSV source files in `publish/metadata/attributes/` and `publish/metadata/personnel/`. Do not edit the auto-generated `.txt` files directly — they are overwritten each time `make_eml_edi()` runs.

### Reserve an EDI package ID

Log in to <https://portal.edirepository.org/>, go to **Tools → Reserve a Package ID**, and note your assigned number (e.g., `edi.1234.1`).

Use `edi.000.1` as a placeholder while developing and testing locally. Switch to your real reserved number only when you are ready to submit.

### Generate EML

**Script:** `publish/make-eml.R`

Fill in the inputs at the top of the script and run it:

```r
library(hrlpub)

# Data table(s) — filenames only; make_eml_edi() reads from data/clean/
data_file_names <- "microhabitat_observations_clean.csv"

# Matching attribute definition file(s) — filenames only;
# make_eml_edi() reads from publish/metadata/attributes/
attributes_file_names <- "attributes_microhabitat_observations.csv"

title       <- "Feather River Microhabitat Observations"
maintenance <- "annually"
edi_number  <- "edi.000.1"   # replace with your reserved number before submitting

hrlpub::make_eml_edi(
  data_file_names       = data_file_names,
  attributes_file_names = attributes_file_names,
  title                 = title,
  maintenance           = maintenance,
  edi_number            = edi_number
)
```

This writes `publish/eml/edi.000.1.xml` (or your actual package number). **No data is sent to EDI at this stage.** Open the XML file to inspect the generated metadata before proceeding.

```r
source("scripts/setup.R")
source("publish/make-eml.R")
```

### Submit to EDI

Once the EML looks correct, add your credentials to your project `.Renviron` file:

```
EDI_USER_ID=your_username
EDI_PASSWORD=your_password
```

Then uncomment and run the submission call in `publish/make-eml.R`:

```r
hrlpub::publish_data_edi(
  path_eml_file       = file.path("publish", "eml", paste0(edi_number, ".xml")),
  publish_type        = "new",        # use "update" for a revised version
  edi_number          = edi_number,
  publish_environment = "staging"     # review on staging before switching to "production"
)
```

Always publish to `staging` first. The EDI staging environment lets you review how your data package will appear before it is publicly visible. Once confirmed, change `publish_environment = "production"` and run again.

For updates to an already-published package, increment the version number (e.g., `edi.1234.2`) and use `publish_type = "update"`.

---

## Running the full pipeline

### Option 1: Script by script (recommended for learning)

Run each stage in order:

```r
source("scripts/setup.R")
source("ingest/read-data-cdec.R")
source("clean/clean-microhabitat-observations.R")
source("qc/qc-microhabitat-observations.R")
source("publish/make-eml.R")
```

### Option 2: Pipeline runner

Runs all stages automatically in sequence:

```r
source("scripts/run_pipeline.R")
```

Or from the terminal:

```bash
Rscript scripts/run_pipeline.R
# Skip stages with flags, e.g.:
Rscript scripts/run_pipeline.R --skip-ingest --skip-clean
```

### Option 3: `{targets}` (recommended for iterative work)

[`{targets}`](https://books.ropensci.org/targets/) tracks dependencies between pipeline stages and only re-runs what has changed. If you update the cleaning logic, only the clean, QC, and publish stages re-run; ingest is skipped if its inputs haven't changed.

```r
targets::tar_make()
```

The pipeline is defined in `_targets.R` at the project root. Use `{targets}` once you are iterating on a real dataset and the overhead of always running every stage becomes a bottleneck.

---

## Starting your own dataset

This repository is a tutorial. To begin a new dataset workflow:

1. Clone the [hrl-edi-template](https://github.com/FlowWest/hrl-edi-template) repository — it provides blank versions of all the metadata templates and runner scripts ready to fill in.
2. Checkout a new branch in your GitHub repository labelled as the EDI number and version (e.g. edi-1245.2) of the publication.
3. Place your raw data in `data/raw/`, or write an ingest script following the pattern in `ingest/read-data-cdec.R`.
4. Write a cleaning script following `clean/clean-microhabitat-observations.R`.
5. Write a QC script following `qc/qc-microhabitat-observations.R`.
6. Fill in `publish/metadata/` and run `publish/make-eml.R` in the staging environment.
7. Fulfill any internal review requirements needed before publishing
8. Run `publish/make-eml.R` in the production environment to publish your data.
9. Announce the release to the Central Data Team with schema highlights and contact info.

## Quick reference

| Task | Command |
|------|---------|
| Restore packages | `source("scripts/setup.R")` |
| Run ingest | `source("ingest/ingest.R"); run_ingest()` |
| Run cleaning | `source("clean/clean.R"); run_clean()` |
| Run QC | `source("qc/qc.R"); run_qc()` |
| Generate EML | `source("publish/make-eml.R")` |
| Run full pipeline | `source("scripts/run_pipeline.R")` |
| Run with targets | `targets::tar_make()` |
